[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: The Elusive, Impossible, Iconic War Photo
[METADATA/INTRO]: The Elusive, Impossible, Iconic War Photo Revisiting the past to unravel the flood of images emerging from the Israel-Hamas War....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: After processing his film, Rubinger felt this photo, of Rabbi Shlomo Goren on the shoulders of jubilant soldiers, was his strongest. 
 Thankfully, Rubinger also showed prints to his wife, Anni, asking her opinion. She selected the iconic photo of the three soldiers. “In her view much more effective than the one of Rabbi Goren,” Rubinger wrote.2 
 The sequence building up to the iconic photo is fascinating — the mood evolves from superficial to something deeper, more lasting. 
 Surprisingly, LIFE passed on Rubinger’s photo. Instead, they selected an image of Egyptians surrendering to Israeli troops for their June 16, 1967 cover.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2017
[TITLE]: Condemned Batista Corporal Receives Last Rites From Priest During Cuban Revolution, 1959 — PiqueShow
[METADATA/INTRO]: Condemned Batista Corporal Receives Last Rites From Priest During Cuban Revolution, 1959 Unfortunately, the path to overthrowing Cuban dictator Fulgencio Batista was not a bloodless one, as this Pulitzer Prize-winning image by photographer Andrew Lopez captures so poignantly....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Condemned Batista Corporal Receives Last Rites From Priest During Cuban Revolution, 1959 
 Unfortunately, the path to overthrowing Cuban dictator Fulgencio Batista was not a bloodless one, as this Pulitzer Prize-winning image by photographer Andrew Lopez captures so poignantly. 
 "5/2/60 - New York: United Press International Newspictures Staff Photographer Andrew Lopez has won the 1960 Pulitzer Prize for Photography for a picture series on Cuban executions, it was announced 5/2. Photo below, one of the series, shows former Batista Army Cpl. Jose Rodriguez (C) receiving last rites from a priest at Matanzas, Cuba, 1/17/59, as a guard ties his hands. Sentenced to death for war crimes, Rodriguez faced a firing squad immediately after trial by a Castro tribunal that lasted exactly one minute."

[TITLE]: Lessons in Nonviolent Palestinian Resistance From the First Intifada: An Interview with Mubarak Awad - In These Times
[METADATA/INTRO]: Lessons in Nonviolent Palestinian Resistance From the First Intifada: An Interview with Mubarak Awad Mubarak Awad was one of the main organizers of the nonviolent resistance during the First Intifada and continues to practice strategic forms of nonviolent resistance to...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Lessons in Nonviolent Palestinian Resistance From the First Intifada: An Interview with Mubarak Awad 
 Mubarak Awad was one of the main organizers of the nonviolent resistance during the First Intifada and continues to practice strategic forms of nonviolent resistance to Israeli occupation. 
 The largest Palestinian uprising in the history of the Israeli occupation is largely forgotten today. In the 1980s, thousands of Palestinians took part in large-scale civil disobedience actions, strikes, pickets, boycotts and sit-ins demanding freedom, later becoming known as the First Intifada, the Arabic word for “a shaking off.” 
 Images of Israeli soldiers clashing with Palestinian teenagers, women and the elderly circulated worldwide as the three major American nightly news broadcasts dedicated more time to the intifada than to any other story. While the “stone thrower” became the dominant image in the later stages of the intifada, vastly underreported were the daily decisions by Palestinians to refuse to cooperate with the Israeli occupation without using weapons.
